title: How to Teach Kids to Help with Herb Prep Safely
meta_description: Teach kids safe kitchen prep with practical tips and the right tools. Discover how herb scissors make herb chopping easy, fun, and safe for young chefs.
html:
Getting kids involved in the kitchen is a great way to build confidence, teach nutrition, and create lasting memories. The trick is to choose safe kitchen tools for kids and to set up a workflow that keeps little hands out of danger while still letting them be hands‑on. Below are step‑by‑step strategies for introducing herb preparation—one of the simplest, most flavorful tasks—without compromising safety.

Start with the Right Tools

The foundation of any kid‑friendly cooking lesson is the equipment. Traditional knives are a no‑go for beginners, but you don’t need a full set of gadgets to make herbs safe and fun. Look for tools that are specifically designed for small hands and that limit the risk of cuts. The Herb Scissors are a perfect example: five‑blade shears that mince cilantro, chives, and basil in a single snip, and they come with a cleaning comb to keep the blades tidy. Because the blades are enclosed in a scissor‑like handle, children can’t accidentally swipe across their fingers the way they might with a chef’s knife.

Set Up a Kid‑Safe Prep Station

Before you even open the herb package, create a dedicated space that removes clutter and hazards. A small, stable cutting board (non‑slip), a bowl for the herbs, and a container for the finished mince keep everything contained. Place the Herb Scissors within easy reach, and demonstrate how the comb is used to clear any stray leaf fragments after each cut. Having a single, purpose‑built tool reduces the temptation to reach for a knife, which is a key safety win.

Teach the Basics of Hand Positioning

Even with kid‑friendly tools, proper hand placement matters. Show your child how to hold the Herb Scissors with their thumb on the top handle and fingers wrapped around the lower handle. Emphasize that the “blade side” should always point away from the body and that the fingers stay clear of the cutting edge. A quick drill—have them open and close the scissors a few times over a piece of paper—helps them get comfortable before they move on to actual herbs.

Break Down the Herb Prep Process

Kids learn best when tasks are broken into bite‑size steps. Here’s a simple workflow you can follow together:

  1. Rinse and dry the herb bundle. Pat it gently with a clean kitchen towel.
  2. Trim any tough stems with kitchen scissors (the same Herb Scissors work well for this).
  3. Gather a handful of leaves and place them in the bowl.
  4. Snip using the Herb Scissors. One swift motion yields a pile of fine herb mince.
  5. Clean the blades with the included comb, then wipe with a damp cloth.

Each step reinforces a safety habit—wet hands stay away from blades, the scissor motion stays controlled, and cleaning the tool keeps it ready for the next use.

Make It Fun and Rewarding

Kids are more likely to repeat safe behavior when it feels like a game. Turn the herb‑mincing session into a timed challenge (e.g., “Can we mince enough cilantro for the salsa in 30 seconds?”) or let them name the herb they’re working with. Praise accurate hand placement and careful snipping more than speed. When the finished mince is added to a dish—like a taco topping or a simple pasta sauce—let them see the direct impact of their effort. That connection fuels enthusiasm for safe kitchen practices.

Transition to More Advanced Tasks Gradually

Once children master the Herb Scissors, you can introduce other safe kitchen tools for kids, such as a child‑size serrated knife for soft fruits or a plastic tongs for flipping. The key is to keep the progression logical: start with tools that have built‑in safety features, then move to items that require a bit more skill under close supervision. Always revisit the core safety principles—handle orientation, finger placement, and controlled motions—each time you add a new tool.

FAQ

What age is appropriate for using Herb Scissors?

Most children ages 5 and up can handle the Herb Scissors comfortably, provided they receive a brief demonstration and adult supervision during the first few uses.

Can the Herb Scissors be used for other vegetables?

Yes, they work well on soft leafy greens like spinach or parsley, but they are not designed for hard vegetables like carrots or onions.

How do I clean the Herb Scissors after each use?

Use the built‑in cleaning comb to dislodge any stuck pieces, then rinse the blades under warm water and dry with a towel. The comb ensures no tiny leaf fragments remain hidden in the blades.
